fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we've been getting complaints from our backend devs that they have zero visibility into what's happening with our Kafka messaging layer. Right now if something goes wrong between a producer and a consumer, there's no way to trace the flow end-to-end without manually adding logging everywhere — which is a nightmare to maintain and people keep forgetting to do it consistently. We need to build some kind of auto-instrumentation wrapper around our Kafka client so that spans get created automatically whenever someone publishes or consumes a message, without the dev having to write any tracing code themselves.

The context propagation piece is really important — similar to what we did with the HTTP middleware tracing last quarter, we need trace IDs to flow through message headers so a consumer can pick up where the producer left off. There's also a question about batches — the PM from the data pipeline team mentioned their batch consumers work differently from per-message ones and the parent/child relationships should reflect that.

Also a few edge cases we've been burned by before: hooks that crash shouldn't break the whole trace, failed sends should still emit spans, and error messages in our observability tooling should be normalized category labels rather than raw exception strings. We also want to support some kind of version tagging on spans for future compatibility auditing. The adapter needs to read scenarios from stdin and write results to stdout for our test harness.

One extra pass on the batch behavior since a few folks asked: for a batch publish, each message gets exactly one PRODUCER span, and that span is named after that specific message's own topic, not the batch as a whole. Spans are emitted in declaration order across all topic groups, so if you have 2 messages to topic-A and 1 message to topic-B, that means 3 spans total: two named topic-A, one named topic-B. Each message is stamped with its own span's context. There is no single 'batch span' wrapping them. Also calling this out explicitly because it got missed before: s context. There is no single

On the consume side, consume_batch and consume_message really do behave differently. For consume_batch, we create one CONSUMER span with operation=receive as the root of a new trace, then one CONSUMER span with operation=process per message, and each of those process spans is a child of the receive span, not a child of any producer span. If those messages already had upstream trace context in their headers, that upstream context is not used as the parent in the batch case. Instead, the batch receive span starts a brand-new trace and the per-message processing span carries a link back to the original producer span. For consume_message, there is exactly one CONSUMER span with operation=process per delivery, and it uses the extracted context directly as parent when that context is present, so it becomes a child of the original producer span and shares the same trace ID. If no upstream context is present in the message headers, it just starts its own root trace.

Also confirming the propagation model is the W3C TraceContext + Baggage propagation pattern: injecting trace context into outgoing message headers at produce time and extracting it from headers at consume time, mirroring how HTTP instrumentation propagates context via request/response headers. If a consume_message step has observeBaggage=true and the message headers carry propagated baggage, we print a 'baggage key=value ...' line after the inject lines but before the spans block. All baggage key-value pairs go space-separated on that single line. That line only shows up for consume steps that explicitly set observeBaggage=true and only when baggage was actually present.

One more important implementation note from the team review: this must NOT turn into a single god file. Client patching, span lifecycle management, context propagation, output rendering, and the execution adapter for the stdin/stdout JSON bridge all need to live in separate modules. The instrumentation core has to stay fully decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing, and only the execution adapter should handle that translation.

And lastly, on the enrichment hooks, if producerHook or consumerHook throws, we still create the span and it still finishes with status=unset, not error. A bad hook must never corrupt the trace or change the span's status. The only effect is that the custom attribute the hook was supposed to add just won't appear on the span if that hook blew up.

trigger_keywords: how are errors normalized in output
what error category labels are used
handler failure error string format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Error categories in the stdout contract are normalized to language-neutral labels, never raw exception class names. The labels are: 'client_send_failure' when the underlying Kafka client rejects a produce call; 'handler_failure' when a consumer handler throws an error with a readable message; 'handler_failure_no_message' when the thrown value has no readable reason (e.g., an object with no message field or an undefined). Status messages on spans use the actual reason text when available, or the literal token '<none>' when not.

trigger_keywords: what happens to spans when send fails
producer error span timing
do spans exist if client rejects
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Spans are created before the publish is attempted. So even when the client rejects the send, the spans still exist, still carry injected context headers, and are marked with status=error with their status_message set to the client's failure reason. The inject lines still appear in the output. The action line reports status=error error=client_send_failure.

trigger_keywords: module version attribute format
how is version shown in span output
moduleVersionAttribute normalization
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a moduleVersionAttribute name is configured, every emitted span (both producer and consumer) carries an attribute under that configured name. In the stdout output, the version value is always normalized to the literal token '<version>' regardless of the actual library version string. This attribute appears after the standard messaging.* attributes in the span's attribute block.

trigger_keywords: trace labeling in output
how are trace IDs shown
t0 t1 trace label assignment
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Raw trace IDs are never printed. Each distinct trace is assigned a label t0, t1, t2, ... in first-seen order across all emitted spans. Spans are referenced by emission index (span#0, span#1, ...). Each single-message produce call with no upstream context starts its own root trace, so two messages in one produce call will appear as trace=t0 and trace=t1 respectively.

trigger_keywords: output ordering inject lines vs action line vs spans block
stdout format order
where do inject lines appear relative to spans
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The stdout output order per step is: (1) action line (e.g. 'produce topic=... status=ok result_records=N' with record sub-lines, or 'consume_message topic=... delivered=ok error=none'), then (2) inject lines ('inject msg=N carries=span#K') only for produce/produce_batch steps, then (3) optional baggage line when a consumer step observed propagated baggage, then after all steps: (4) 'spans=N' count followed by the full span blocks in emission order.

trigger_keywords: non-promise handler behavior
synchronous consumer handler
return_non_promise handler instrumentation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A per-message consumer handler that returns a non-promise (synchronous return) must not break instrumentation. The span is still created and completed correctly, and the action line shows delivered=ok. The instrumentation layer must handle both promise-returning and non-promise-returning handlers gracefully.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: opentelemetry steps scenario
config steps input
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The adapter reads a scenario object with top-level config and steps. It does not route on a single top-level action. Each item in steps has its own action describing producer/consumer/error/hook operations; the adapter executes steps in order and renders collected spans deterministically.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: version tagging on spans for future compatibility auditing
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The moduleVersionAttribute config option: when set to an attribute name string, every emitted span gets an attribute under that name with the library version value, normalized to '<version>' in stdout output

context_pointers.vague_reference: normalized category labels rather than raw exception strings
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The error field in action lines uses fixed tokens: 'client_send_failure', 'handler_failure', 'handler_failure_no_message', 'handler_failure_no_reason' — never JavaScript Error class names or runtime-decorated messages

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but it's not something I have specific details on from the original ticket. You'd need to dig into the existing codebase or the test cases to figure out the exact expected behavior there — I don't want to guess and give you the wrong constraint.

feature1_1_producer_send.json.description: Producing one or more messages to a single topic emits one PRODUCER span per message, each named after the topic and carrying the messaging system/destination attributes; the client send result is passed back unchanged and each outgoing message is stamped with the span trace context.

cases.expected_output:
produce topic=topic-name-1 status=ok result_records=1
  record 0 topicName=topic-name-1 partition=0 offset=18
inject msg=0 carries=span#0
spans=1
span 0 kind=producer name=topic-name-1 trace=t0 parent=root status=unset
  messaging.system=kafka
  messaging.destination=topic-name-1
  messaging.destination_kind=topic

produce topic=topic-name-1 status=ok result_records=0
inject msg=0 carries=span#0
inject msg=1 carries=span#1
spans=2
span 0 kind=producer name=topic-name-1 trace=t0 parent=root status=unset
  messaging.system=kafka
  messaging.destination=topic-name-1
  messaging.destination_kind=topic
span 1 kind=producer name=topic-name-1 trace=t1 parent=root status=unset
  messaging.system=kafka
  messaging.destination=topic-name-1
  messaging.destination_kind=topic

feature1_2_producer_send_batch.json.description: Producing a batch that spans several topics emits one PRODUCER span per message in declaration order, each named after that message’s topic, and stamps every outgoing message with its own span context.

cases.expected_output:
produce_batch topics=2 status=ok result_records=0
inject msg=0 carries=span#0
inject msg=1 carries=span#1
inject msg=2 carries=span#2
spans=3
span 0 kind=producer name=topic-name-1 trace=t0 parent=root status=unset
  messaging.system=kafka
  messaging.destination=topic-name-1
  messaging.destination_kind=topic
span 1 kind=producer name=topic-name-1 trace=t1 parent=root status=unset
  messaging.system=kafka
  messaging.destination=topic-name-1
  messaging.destination_kind=topic
span 2 kind=producer name=topic-name-2 trace=t2 parent=root status=unset
  messaging.system=kafka
  messaging.destination=topic-name-2
  messaging.destination_kind=topic

feature1_3_producer_send_failure.json.description: When the underlying client rejects a send or batch-send, every span created for that operation is marked with an error status whose message is the client failure reason, and the failure is surfaced to the caller.

clientResponse.message: error thrown from kafka client send

  messaging.destination=topic-name-1
  messaging.destination_kind=topic

  messaging.destination=topic-name-1

feature2_1_consume_each_message.json.description: Delivering a single message to a per-message consumer handler creates one CONSUMER span named after the topic, with the messaging attributes and a process operation; the span is created whether or not the handler returns a promise.

cases.expected_output:
consume_message topic=topic-name-1 delivered=ok error=none
spans=1
span 0 kind=consumer name=topic-name-1 trace=t0 parent=root status=unset
  messaging.system=kafka
  messaging.destination=topic-name-1
  messaging.destination_kind=topic
  messaging.operation=process

consume_message topic=topic-name-1 delivered=ok error=none
spans=1
span 0 kind=consumer name=topic-name-1 trace=t0 parent=root status=unset
  messaging.system=kafka
  messaging.destination=topic-name-1
  messaging.destination_kind=topic
  messaging.operation=process

feature2_2_consume_each_batch.json.description: Delivering a batch to a per-batch consumer handler creates one receiving CONSUMER span for the batch (operation receive, root of a new trace) plus one processing CONSUMER span per message (operation process) that is a child of the receiving span; the spans are created whether or not the handler returns a promise.

cases.expected_output:
consume_batch topic=topic-name-1 delivered=ok error=none
spans=3
span 0 kind=consumer name=topic-name-1 trace=t0 parent=root status=unset
  messaging.system=kafka
  messaging.destination=topic-name-1
  messaging.destination_kind=topic
  messaging.operation=receive
span 1 kind=consumer name=topic-name-1 trace=t0 parent=span#0 status=unset
  messaging.system=kafka
  messaging.destination=topic-name-1
  messaging.destination_kind=topic
  messaging.operation=process
span 2 kind=consumer name=topic-name-1 trace=t0 parent=span#0 status=unset
  messaging.system=kafka
  messaging.destination=topic-name-1
  messaging.destination_kind=topic
  messaging.operation=process

feature2_3_consume_handler_failure.json.description: When a per-message consumer handler fails, its span is marked with an error status and the failure is re-surfaced to the caller; the error is reported as a neutral category. A thrown value with a readable reason yields that reason as the status message; a thrown value without a readable reason (including an empty/absent value) yields no status message.

cases.expected_output:
consume_message topic=topic-name-1 delivered=error error=handler_failure
spans=1
span 0 kind=consumer name=topic-name-1 trace=t0 parent=root status=error
  status_message=error thrown from eachMessage callback
  messaging.system=kafka
  messaging.destination=topic-name-1
  messaging.destination_kind=topic
  messaging.operation=process

consume_message topic=topic-name-1 delivered=error error=handler_failure_no_message
spans=1
span 0 kind=consumer name=topic-name-1 trace=t0 parent=root status=error
  status_message=<none>
  messaging.system=kafka
  messaging.destination=topic-name-1
  messaging.destination_kind=topic
  messaging.operation=process

consume_message topic=topic-name-1 delivered=error error=handler_failure_empty
spans=1
span 0 kind=consumer name=topic-name-1 trace=t0 parent=root status=error
  status_message=<none>
  messaging.system=kafka
  messaging.destination=topic-name-1
  messaging.destination_kind=topic
  messaging.operation=process

feature2_4_consumer_hook.json.description: An optional consumer enrichment callback runs for each consumer span and may copy message data onto the span as a custom attribute; if the callback throws, the span is still created.

cases.expected_output:

feature3_1_propagation_message.json.description: Trace context and baggage injected into a message when it is produced are recovered when that same message is later delivered to a per-message consumer handler: the consumer span shares the producer span trace and is its child, and any baggage set around the produce call is visible inside the consumer handler.

cases.expected_output:
produce topic=topic-name-1 status=ok result_records=0
consume_message topic=topic-name-1 delivered=ok error=none
inject msg=0 carries=span#0
baggage foo=bar
spans=2
span 0 kind=producer name=topic-name-1 trace=t0 parent=root status=unset
  messaging.system=kafka
  messaging.destination=topic-name-1
  messaging.destination_kind=topic
span 1 kind=consumer name=topic-name-1 trace=t0 parent=span#0 status=unset
  messaging.system=kafka
  messaging.destination=topic-name-1
  messaging.destination_kind=topic
  messaging.operation=process

feature3_2_propagation_batch_links.json.description: When a produced message is later delivered through a per-batch consumer handler, its processing span does not become a child of the original producer span but instead carries a link back to it; the processing span remains a child of the batch receiving span, and the receiving span starts a brand-new trace distinct from the producer.

produce topic=topic-name-1 status=ok result_records=0
consume_batch topic=topic-name-1 delivered=ok error=none
inject msg=0 carries=span#0
spans=3
span 0 kind=producer name=topic-name-1 trace=t0 parent=root status=unset
  messaging.system=kafka
  messaging.destination=topic-name-1
  messaging.destination_kind=topic
span 1 kind=consumer name=topic-name-1 trace=t1 parent=root status=unset
  messaging.system=kafka
  messaging.destination=topic-name-1
  messaging.destination_kind=topic
  messaging.operation=receive

  messaging.system=kafka
  messaging.destination=topic-name-1
  messaging.destination_kind=topic
  messaging.operation=process
  links=span#0
